Content_Sketch: News calls it digital gold - you sit in a cafe staring at your coffee knowing you built it
VO_Draft_EN: The news calls it digital gold. You're sitting in a cafe. Your laptop is open. The price is on the screen. You know who built it. You know exactly who. You can't say a word.

Content_Sketch: 1 million coins sitting untouched - worth tens of billions - the largest fortune no one has ever touched
VO_Draft_EN: One million coins sitting in a wallet. Untouched. Worth tens of billions now. The largest fortune no one has ever moved. It doesn't belong to anyone. Because the person who owns it doesn't exist.

Content_Sketch: You created digital gold - then you stood up and walked away
VO_Draft_EN: You created digital gold. Then you stood up and walked away.

Content_Sketch: Late nights coding alone - nobody asked you to fix money - but you cannot stop thinking about it
VO_Draft_EN: It starts in 2007. Late nights. A desk. A screen. Nobody asked you to fix money. Nobody asked you to redesign how trust works between strangers. But you can't stop thinking about it. The problem is simple. Every transaction needs a middleman. A bank. A processor. Someone you have to trust. You want to remove that someone.

Content_Sketch: If you fail nobody knows - if you succeed everything changes
VO_Draft_EN: If you fail, nothing happens. Nobody knows your name. Nobody knows you tried. If you succeed, everything changes. And you still won't have a name.

Content_Sketch: Write 9-page whitepaper on Halloween night - send to cryptography mailing list - mostly silence
VO_Draft_EN: October 31st, 2008. Halloween. You finish the whitepaper. Nine pages. You title it: Bitcoin: A Peer-to-Peer Electronic Cash System. You send it to a cryptography mailing list. A few dozen people read it. Most say nothing. One or two say it won't work. You don't argue. You just keep writing code.

Content_Sketch: Register bitcoin.org under fake name - create P2P Foundation profile with symbolic birthday
VO_Draft_EN: You register bitcoin.org. Fake name. Fake address. You create a profile on the P2P Foundation. Birthday: April 5th, 1975. April 5th, 1933 - the day the US government banned citizens from owning gold. 1975 - the year they gave it back. Nobody notices.

Content_Sketch: You live alone with this secret - no colleagues no mentor - no one
VO_Draft_EN: You have no team. No funding. No advisor. No one to tell when the code compiles at 3 AM. You live alone with this. Not because you have to. Because you understand that what you're building must never have an owner.

Content_Sketch: Mine Genesis Block - embed newspaper headline about bank bailouts
VO_Draft_EN: January 3rd, 2009. You press enter. The first block is mined. Block zero. The genesis block. You embed a headline from that morning's Times of London: Chancellor on brink of second bailout for banks. It's not decoration. It's a timestamp. And a reason.

Content_Sketch: Send 10 BTC to Hal Finney - first transaction ever - worth nothing
VO_Draft_EN: Nine days later. You send ten coins to a man named Hal Finney. He downloaded the software the day it launched. He's the first person on Earth who believed this could work. Ten coins. Worth exactly nothing.

Content_Sketch: People start using it - not because they trust you - because they trust the system
VO_Draft_EN: Slowly, people start using it. Not because they trust you. They don't know you. They trust the math. They trust the code. That's exactly what you wanted. And that's exactly what will erase you.

Content_Sketch: 10000 BTC for 2 pizzas - nobody realizes they are holding gold
VO_Draft_EN: May 22nd, 2010. Someone buys two pizzas for ten thousand coins. Ten thousand coins. Two pizzas. Nobody laughs. Because nobody realizes what they're holding. You do.

Content_Sketch: WikiLeaks accepts Bitcoin - you warn about kicking a hornet's nest
VO_Draft_EN: Then WikiLeaks starts accepting it. The attention is enormous. You post on the forum: you've kicked a hornet's nest. Too soon. Too fast. You didn't build this to become political news. But you can't control what people do with something you gave away.

Content_Sketch: Either you leave or you destroy it - the project is only decentralized when its creator walks away
VO_Draft_EN: And here's the thing you understand now. If you stay, you're the center. If you're the center, it isn't decentralized. If it isn't decentralized, it's just another system with a name at the top. The only way your invention survives is if you don't.

Content_Sketch: Hand over source code and network alert key to Gavin Andresen
VO_Draft_EN: You start handing things over. The source code. The network alert key. You give them to Gavin Andresen. He's the closest thing this project has to a successor. You don't tell him why.

Content_Sketch: Email to Mike Hearn and final email to Gavin
VO_Draft_EN: April 23rd, 2011. You email a developer named Mike Hearn. You write: I've moved on to other things. It's in good hands. Three days later, you email Gavin. Your last message ever. You write: I wish you wouldn't keep talking about me as a mysterious shadowy figure.

Content_Sketch: Gavin announces CIA presentation - you read it - the moment of decision
VO_Draft_EN: The next day, April 27th. Gavin posts on the forum. He announces he's been invited to present Bitcoin at the CIA. You read that line.

Content_Sketch: You sit there a little longer than usual - then close the laptop and walk away
VO_Draft_EN: You don't panic. You don't run. You just sit there a little longer than usual. With the keyboard. With the screen. With the thing you spent four years building. Then you close the laptop. Nobody forced you. Nobody chased you. You just stood up. And walked away.

Content_Sketch: Bitcoin price climbs - digital gold becomes real
VO_Draft_EN: The price starts climbing. One dollar. Ten. A hundred. A thousand. Ten thousand. Sixty thousand. Your coins - one million of them - sit untouched. You are the richest person in the world. On paper. On a paper that doesn't have your name on it.

Content_Sketch: The world keeps trying to find you - every few years someone becomes you
VO_Draft_EN: The world keeps trying to find you. You keep not existing. Every few years, someone becomes you. A retired engineer in California gets ambushed by reporters outside his house. He says he's never heard of Bitcoin. He's telling the truth. A man in Australia goes to court claiming he built it. The court disagrees. He is not you. And every time someone claims your name - you let them.

Content_Sketch: Hal Finney dies 2014 - you cannot attend his funeral
VO_Draft_EN: Hal Finney dies in 2014. ALS. The first person who ever received your coins. The first person who believed. You can't send flowers. You can't attend the funeral. You can't even post a message saying goodbye. Because the moment you speak - you destroy everything you built.

Content_Sketch: Digital gold is everywhere - the person who created it is nowhere
VO_Draft_EN: Digital gold is everywhere now. In portfolios. In legislation. In arguments at dinner tables. The person who created it is nowhere. You sit in a cafe. Someone at the next table says Bitcoin is the future. You nod. Politely.

Content_Sketch: Stranger asks where Satoshi is now - you sip coffee and say nothing
VO_Draft_EN: The same cafe. The same laptop. The same coffee. A stranger leans over. I wonder where Satoshi is, he says. You take a sip. You don't answer.

Content_Sketch: The price of creating digital gold is that you can never claim it as yours
VO_Draft_EN: The price of creating digital gold. Is that you can never claim it as yours.
